'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23年4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2年9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7年2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11年2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23年4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2年9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7年2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11年2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|3}}

== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23年4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2年9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7年2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11年2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:

In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23年4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2年9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7年2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11年2月13日条</ref>

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23年4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2年9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7年2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7年2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
== References ==
{{Reflist|3}}

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}}). {{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=draft}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理|12=draft}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=draft}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い|12=draft}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|12=draft}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使|12=draft}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平|12=draft}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀|12=draft}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国|12=draft}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国|12=draft}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|12=draft}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Silla envoy]] [[Category:Asuka period diplomacy]] [[Category:Asuka period foreign visitors]] [[Category:Nara period diplomacy]] [[Category:Nara period foreign visitors]] [[Category:Silla relations]] [[Category:Ancient Japan-Korea relations]] [[Category:Japan-China relations]] [[Category:Historical diplomatic missions to Japan]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Silla envoy]] [[ja:新羅使]] [[en:Silla envoy]]

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft}} to {{ill|Wa|ja|倭|12=draft}} or {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|12=draft}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|12=draft}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=draft}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft}} and {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|12=draft}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=draft}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft}} regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika Era|ja|大化|12=draft}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=draft}}, scholar {{ill|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|ja|高向玄理|12=draft}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=draft}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い|12=draft}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|12=draft}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|ja|遣唐使|12=draft}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平|12=draft}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|ja|宝亀|12=draft}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|ja|遠江国|12=draft}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国|12=draft}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|12=draft}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

revision.text:
'''新羅使'''（しらぎし）とは、[[新羅]]より[[倭]]あるいは[[日本]]を訪問した公式の使節である。
== 概要 == 6世紀末、西暦[[562年]]に[[新羅]]が[[任那]]の[[官家]]を占領し、滅亡させた時<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref>から始まっている。『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九、二十によると、
{{quotation| #秋七月（ふみづき）の己巳（つちのとのみ）の朔（ついたち）の日に、新羅、使（つかひ）を遣（まだ）して、調賦（みつきもの）を献（たてまつ）る<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。 #冬十一月（しもつき）に、新羅、使（つかひ）を遣（まだ）して献（ものたてまつ）り、幷（あはせ）て調賦（みつきもの）を貢（たてまつ）る<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年11月条</ref> #（[[574年]]）十一月（しもつき）に、新羅、使（つかひ）を遣（まだ）して、調（みつき）を進（たてまつ）る<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年11月条</ref>。}}
とあり、とりわけ[[575年]]（[[敏達天皇]]4年6月）には、「幷（あはせ）て[[多多羅]]（たたら）・[[須奈羅]]（すなら）・[[和陀]]（わだ）・[[発鬼]]（はちくゐ）、四つの邑（むら）の調（みつき）を進（たてまつ）る」とある<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref>、この地は[[529年]]（[[継体天皇]]23年4月）の「多多羅（たたら）・須那羅（すなら）・和多（わた）・費智（ほち）」の4つの村（すき）に当たり<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23年4月条</ref>、旧任那地域で[[近江毛野]]が失った地に当たる。新羅は任那滅亡後、この4村からの貢納を代理で行う義務を負ったのだという。
大和政権は[[百済]]や[[高句麗]]と同盟を結んでおり、新羅は自国の調と任那の調を同時に貢上する朝貢の形式をとった。[[611年]]（[[推古天皇]]18年7月）に新羅・任那の使人が到着した際にも、大和政権は[[額田部比羅夫|額田部連比羅夫]]・[[膳大伴|膳臣大伴]]の2名に、「各国」の荘馬（かざりうま）の長（おさ）を任せ、「二つの国」の使者に入国の目的を弁じさせるというふうに<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条、9月条、10月8日条、9日条、17日条、23日条</ref>、それぞれの「国」の饗応をするという形式をとり続けた。
その後、[[642年]]（[[斉明天皇|皇極天皇]]元年、百済[[義慈王]]2年）、[[百済]]が旧任那の中心地域を新羅から奪うと、任那の調は百済が納めることとなり、[[645年]]（[[大化]]元年七月）に高句麗・百済・新羅の3国が調を進上した際には、
{{quotation|百済の調（みつき）の使、任那（みまな）の使（つかひ）を兼領（あづか）りて、任那の調（みつき）を進（たてまつ）る。<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
とある。この結果、翌[[646年]]（大化2年九月）には、新羅からは代わりに人質をとろうということになり、
{{quotation|[[小徳]]（せうとく）[[高向玄理|高向博士黒麻呂]]（たかむく の はかせ くろまろ）を新羅（しらき）に遣（つかは）して、質（むはかり）貢（たてまつ）らしむ。遂（つひ）に任那（みまな）の調（みつき）を罷（や）めしむ。<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2年9月条</ref>}}
となった。この結果、[[647年]]に[[武烈王|金春秋]]が人質として日本に送られてきた。新羅の調はそのまま継続され、647年（大化3年）に高句麗の使いとともに調を貢上している<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化3年1月15日条</ref>。
[[663年]]の[[白村江の戦い]]で新羅との国交は中断したが、[[668年]]（[[天智天皇]]7年9月）に再開すると<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条、26日条、29日条</ref>、再度活発になり、頻繁な使節の往来があった。この時期、30年間日本と唐との外交交渉は前述の白村江の影響で全く行われておらず、当初、唐と同盟（朝貢関係）を結び、のちに半島統一のために離叛した新羅との利害の一致を見たため、新羅の承認のもと、（任那の調同様に）滅亡した百済・高句麗の使節が派遣されてもいた。唐の留学生･僧が新羅を経由したほか、新羅留学生･僧も少なくなく、日本の政治･制度･文化面で、日本の律令体制の確立期に新羅使の果たした役割は大きなものであり、その航路は大陸の文物の主要な輸入路でもあった{{要出典|date=2020年3月}}。
その回数は[[遣唐使]]を凌駕し、8 - 9世紀には 20回を数え、日本からの[[遣新羅使]]は17回に及んでいる。8世紀には7世紀以来の新羅との朝貢を継続しようとする朝廷と、唐と安定した関係を築くことに成功し、日本との対等関係を求める新羅との政治対立が続き、[[渤海 (国)|渤海]]との関係も相まって、両国の関係は悪化した。唐との国交を回復した新羅は、[[735年]]（[[天平]]7年）に「王城国」と国号を改称し、日本との対等な外交を求めてきたが、勝手に国号を変更したことを日本側に叱責され、新羅からの使者は追い返されている<ref>続日本紀 第12巻 天平7年2月27日条</ref>。その結果、[[759年]]（[[天平宝字]]3年）の[[藤原仲麻呂]]の[[新羅征討計画]]<ref>『続日本紀』廃帝 淳仁天皇 天平宝字3年6月18日条、9月19日条</ref>が浮上するにいたったが、新羅側が商業上の利益を優先したため、両国間の貿易は発展した。
正規の国交は[[779年]]([[宝亀]]10年)までであり、その経緯は『続日本紀』巻第三十五、三十六に以下のように述べられている。
#宝亀10年2月、[[大宰少監]]・[[正六位上]]の[[下道長人|下道朝臣長人]]（しもつみち の あそみ ながひと）が遣唐[[判官]]の[[海上三狩|海上真人三狩]]（うなかみ の まひと みかり）らを迎えにゆくため、遣新羅使に任命された<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年2月13日条</ref>。 #7月、下道朝臣長人が遣唐使の判官の海上真人三狩らを連れて帰国したという大宰府からの報告があった<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年7月10日条</ref>。 #10月に光仁天皇の大宰府への詔があった（新羅使の金蘭孫が正月に調を貢献した理由を調査させ、元通り国交を開こうとした）<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年10月9日条</ref>。 #同月、大宰府に勅令を出して、唐からの客である高鶴林ら5人と新羅の貢納使を入京させた<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年10月17日条</ref>。 #翌年正月（[[780年]]）に新羅使は拝賀し、今まで外敵侵入があって入朝できなかった、また（先にあげたように）海上三狩を見つけたので送迎して帰国させた、と報告した。これに対し、[[大伴伯麻呂|大伴宿禰伯麻呂]]（おおとも の すくね おじまろ）は天皇の詔として、新羅が無礼であったため、近年は使者を追い返していたが、今回は貢ぎ物を納め、新年を祝賀し、三狩を送ってきたことに礼を言う、と伝えた<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀11年1月2日、5日条</ref>。 #2月、新羅使が帰国するにあたって、さらにだめ押しをし、天平勝宝4年6月17日条（[[752年]]、言葉や上表文で奏上すること）や天平宝字4年9月16日条（[[760年]]、相当の身分の人を使者にすること）といった注意をしたように、「上表文」を持参して入国するように、さもなくば、大宰府や対馬の守備兵に追い返させる、と伝えた<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀11年2月15日条</ref>。
とあるのが最後になった。
その後、9世紀になると日羅両国の関係は急速に冷却化し、820年（[[弘仁]]11年）に[[遠江国]]・[[駿河国]]で新羅人700人の叛乱が起こると<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11年2月13日条</ref>、824年（天長元年）には、新羅系の帰化人のすべてを陸奥国の空き地に移住させ、以降の新羅人を強制帰国させるようになり、帰化を一切認めなくなった。
== 貿易品目 == 人参・松の実・蜂蜜や食器・絨緞 などの自国製品のほかに、唐・西域・南海からの香料、顔料、染料や薬品類なども取引された。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|3}}
== 参考文献 == * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p610、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1966年 * 『日本書紀』（三） - （五）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年、1995年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）・（下）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『続日本紀』3 - 5 新日本古典文学大系14 - 16 岩波書店、1992年 - 1998年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（中）・（下）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年、1995年 * 『日本後紀』全現代語訳（下）、講談社学術文庫、森田悌：訳、2007年 * 『日本の歴史2 古代国家の成立』、[[直木孝次郎]]：著、[[中央公論社]]、1965年 * 『日本の歴史3 奈良の都』、[[青木和夫]]：著、[[中央公論社]]、1965年 * 『渡来氏族の謎』[[祥伝社]]新書、加藤謙吉：著、2017年 * 歴史読本臨時増刊入門シリーズ『日本古代史の基礎知識』[[新人物往来社]]、1992年より、「新羅と日本」、文：中野高行
== 関連項目 == * [[遣渤海使]] * [[遣耽羅使]] * [[渤海使]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[新羅使]] to [[Silla envoy]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''新羅使'''（しらぎし）とは、[[新羅]]より[[倭]]あるいは[[日本]]を訪問した公式の使節である。
== 概要 == 6世紀末、西暦[[562年]]に[[新羅]]が[[任那]]の[[官家]]を占領し、滅亡させた時<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref>から始まっている。『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九、二十によると、
{{quotation| #秋七月（ふみづき）の己巳（つちのとのみ）の朔（ついたち）の日に、新羅、使（つかひ）を遣（まだ）して、調賦（みつきもの）を献（たてまつ）る<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。 #冬十一月（しもつき）に、新羅、使（つかひ）を遣（まだ）して献（ものたてまつ）り、幷（あはせ）て調賦（みつきもの）を貢（たてまつ）る<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年11月条</ref> #（[[574年]]）十一月（しもつき）に、新羅、使（つかひ）を遣（まだ）して、調（みつき）を進（たてまつ）る<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年11月条</ref>。}}
とあり、とりわけ[[575年]]（[[敏達天皇]]4年6月）には、「幷（あはせ）て[[多多羅]]（たたら）・[[須奈羅]]（すなら）・[[和陀]]（わだ）・[[発鬼]]（はちくゐ）、四つの邑（むら）の調（みつき）を進（たてまつ）る」とある<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4年6月条</ref>、この地は[[529年]]（[[継体天皇]]23年4月）の「多多羅（たたら）・須那羅（すなら）・和多（わた）・費智（ほち）」の4つの村（すき）に当たり<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23年4月条</ref>、旧任那地域で[[近江毛野]]が失った地に当たる。新羅は任那滅亡後、この4村からの貢納を代理で行う義務を負ったのだという。
大和政権は[[百済]]や[[高句麗]]と同盟を結んでおり、新羅は自国の調と任那の調を同時に貢上する朝貢の形式をとった。[[611年]]（[[推古天皇]]18年7月）に新羅・任那の使人が到着した際にも、大和政権は[[額田部比羅夫|額田部連比羅夫]]・[[膳大伴|膳臣大伴]]の2名に、「各国」の荘馬（かざりうま）の長（おさ）を任せ、「二つの国」の使者に入国の目的を弁じさせるというふうに<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18年7月条、9月条、10月8日条、9日条、17日条、23日条</ref>、それぞれの「国」の饗応をするという形式をとり続けた。
その後、[[642年]]（[[斉明天皇|皇極天皇]]元年、百済[[義慈王]]2年）、[[百済]]が旧任那の中心地域を新羅から奪うと、任那の調は百済が納めることとなり、[[645年]]（[[大化]]元年七月）に高句麗・百済・新羅の3国が調を進上した際には、
{{quotation|百済の調（みつき）の使、任那（みまな）の使（つかひ）を兼領（あづか）りて、任那の調（みつき）を進（たてまつ）る。<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
とある。この結果、翌[[646年]]（大化2年九月）には、新羅からは代わりに人質をとろうということになり、
{{quotation|[[小徳]]（せうとく）[[高向玄理|高向博士黒麻呂]]（たかむく の はかせ くろまろ）を新羅（しらき）に遣（つかは）して、質（むはかり）貢（たてまつ）らしむ。遂（つひ）に任那（みまな）の調（みつき）を罷（や）めしむ。<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2年9月条</ref>}}
となった。この結果、[[647年]]に[[武烈王|金春秋]]が人質として日本に送られてきた。新羅の調はそのまま継続され、647年（大化3年）に高句麗の使いとともに調を貢上している<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化3年1月15日条</ref>。
[[663年]]の[[白村江の戦い]]で新羅との国交は中断したが、[[668年]]（[[天智天皇]]7年9月）に再開すると<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7年9月12日条、26日条、29日条</ref>、再度活発になり、頻繁な使節の往来があった。この時期、30年間日本と唐との外交交渉は前述の白村江の影響で全く行われておらず、当初、唐と同盟（朝貢関係）を結び、のちに半島統一のために離叛した新羅との利害の一致を見たため、新羅の承認のもと、（任那の調同様に）滅亡した百済・高句麗の使節が派遣されてもいた。唐の留学生･僧が新羅を経由したほか、新羅留学生･僧も少なくなく、日本の政治･制度･文化面で、日本の律令体制の確立期に新羅使の果たした役割は大きなものであり、その航路は大陸の文物の主要な輸入路でもあった{{要出典|date=2020年3月}}。
その回数は[[遣唐使]]を凌駕し、8 - 9世紀には 20回を数え、日本からの[[遣新羅使]]は17回に及んでいる。8世紀には7世紀以来の新羅との朝貢を継続しようとする朝廷と、唐と安定した関係を築くことに成功し、日本との対等関係を求める新羅との政治対立が続き、[[渤海 (国)|渤海]]との関係も相まって、両国の関係は悪化した。唐との国交を回復した新羅は、[[735年]]（[[天平]]7年）に「王城国」と国号を改称し、日本との対等な外交を求めてきたが、勝手に国号を変更したことを日本側に叱責され、新羅からの使者は追い返されている<ref>続日本紀 第12巻 天平7年2月27日条</ref>。その結果、[[759年]]（[[天平宝字]]3年）の[[藤原仲麻呂]]の[[新羅征討計画]]<ref>『続日本紀』廃帝 淳仁天皇 天平宝字3年6月18日条、9月19日条</ref>が浮上するにいたったが、新羅側が商業上の利益を優先したため、両国間の貿易は発展した。
正規の国交は[[779年]]([[宝亀]]10年)までであり、その経緯は『続日本紀』巻第三十五、三十六に以下のように述べられている。
#宝亀10年2月、[[大宰少監]]・[[正六位上]]の[[下道長人|下道朝臣長人]]（しもつみち の あそみ ながひと）が遣唐[[判官]]の[[海上三狩|海上真人三狩]]（うなかみ の まひと みかり）らを迎えにゆくため、遣新羅使に任命された<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年2月13日条</ref>。 #7月、下道朝臣長人が遣唐使の判官の海上真人三狩らを連れて帰国したという大宰府からの報告があった<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年7月10日条</ref>。 #10月に光仁天皇の大宰府への詔があった（新羅使の金蘭孫が正月に調を貢献した理由を調査させ、元通り国交を開こうとした）<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年10月9日条</ref>。 #同月、大宰府に勅令を出して、唐からの客である高鶴林ら5人と新羅の貢納使を入京させた<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀10年10月17日条</ref>。 #翌年正月（[[780年]]）に新羅使は拝賀し、今まで外敵侵入があって入朝できなかった、また（先にあげたように）海上三狩を見つけたので送迎して帰国させた、と報告した。これに対し、[[大伴伯麻呂|大伴宿禰伯麻呂]]（おおとも の すくね おじまろ）は天皇の詔として、新羅が無礼であったため、近年は使者を追い返していたが、今回は貢ぎ物を納め、新年を祝賀し、三狩を送ってきたことに礼を言う、と伝えた<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀11年1月2日、5日条</ref>。 #2月、新羅使が帰国するにあたって、さらにだめ押しをし、天平勝宝4年6月17日条（[[752年]]、言葉や上表文で奏上すること）や天平宝字4年9月16日条（[[760年]]、相当の身分の人を使者にすること）といった注意をしたように、「上表文」を持参して入国するように、さもなくば、大宰府や対馬の守備兵に追い返させる、と伝えた<ref>『続日本紀』光仁天皇 宝亀11年2月15日条</ref>。
とあるのが最後になった。
その後、9世紀になると日羅両国の関係は急速に冷却化し、820年（[[弘仁]]11年）に[[遠江国]]・[[駿河国]]で新羅人700人の叛乱が起こると<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11年2月13日条</ref>、824年（天長元年）には、新羅系の帰化人のすべてを陸奥国の空き地に移住させ、以降の新羅人を強制帰国させるようになり、帰化を一切認めなくなった。
== 貿易品目 == 人参・松の実・蜂蜜や食器・絨緞 などの自国製品のほかに、唐・西域・南海からの香料、顔料、染料や薬品類なども取引された。

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55534500]]
Wikidata name: Silla missions to Japan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].

== Overview ==

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:

{{quotation|
# On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|qq=|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|qq=|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|qq=|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}

This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:

{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|qq=|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].

== Overview ==

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:

{{quotation|
# On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}

This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:

{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55534500]]
Wikidata name: Silla missions to Japan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].

== Overview ==

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:

{{quotation|
# On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}

This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:

{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

Wikidata name: Silla missions to Japan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].

== Overview ==

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:

{{quotation|
# On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}

This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:

{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].

== Overview ==

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:

{{quotation|
# On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}

This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:

{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
