In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}

This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:

{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:

# On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].

== Overview ==

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:

{{quotation|
# On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref>
# In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>

In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:

{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}

This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:

{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}

In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>

== Later Developments ==

The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>

== Trade Items ==

Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

{{wikidata link|Q55534500}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Diplomacy in the Asuka Period]] [[Category:Diplomacy of the Nara Period]] [[Category:Foreign missions to Japan in pre-modern times]] [[Category:Foreigners in the Asuka Period]] [[Category:Foreigners in the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Japan–China Relations]] [[Category:International Relations of Silla]] [[Category:Japan–Korea Relations in Antiquity]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55534500]] Wikidata name: Silla missions to Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "pages with conflicting qids" to "qq"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55534500]] Wikidata name: Silla missions to Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to [[:en:Wa (name of Japan)|Wa]] or [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when [[:en:Silla|Silla]] occupied and destroyed the [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Era]]), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar [[:en:Takamuko no Kuromaro|Takamu-no-Kuro Maro]], to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the [[:en:Battle of Baekgang|Battle of Baekgang]] in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese missions to Tang China]], with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]]). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of [[:en:Hōki|Hōki]]). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] and [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] in 820 CE (11th year of [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]]).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
== References ==
{{Reflist|3}}

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Japanese missions to Balhae|ja|遣渤海使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese missions to Balhae|1=Japanese missions to Balhae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642473}} * {{ill|Japanese missions to Tamna|ja|遣耽羅使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese missions to Tamna|1=Japanese missions to Tamna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642475}} * {{ill|Balhae envoys|ja|渤海使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Balhae envoys|1=Balhae missions to Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8015147}}

== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika Era}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamu-no-Kuro Maro}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|WD=Q262529|lt=Japanese missions to Tang China}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|WD=Q18520676|lt=Hōki}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
== References ==
{{Reflist|3}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55534500]] Wikidata name: Silla missions to Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} to {{ill|Wa (name of Japan)|en|Wa (name of Japan)|WD=Q1378053|lt=Wa}} or {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika Era}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamu-no-Kuro Maro}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|WD=Q262529|lt=Japanese missions to Tang China}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|WD=Q18520676|lt=Hōki}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
== References ==

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Japanese missions to Balhae|ja|遣渤海使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese missions to Balhae|1=Japanese missions to Balhae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642473}} * {{ill|Japanese missions to Tamna|ja|遣耽羅使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese missions to Tamna|1=Japanese missions to Tamna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642475}} * {{ill|Balhae envoys|ja|渤海使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Balhae envoys|1=Balhae missions to Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8015147}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]]
'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} to {{ill|Wa (name of Japan)|en|Wa (name of Japan)|WD=Q1378053|lt=Wa}} or {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika Era}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamu-no-Kuro Maro}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|WD=Q262529|lt=Japanese missions to Tang China}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|WD=Q18520676|lt=Hōki}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
== References ==

'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} to {{ill|Wa (name of Japan)|en|Wa (name of Japan)|WD=Q1378053|lt=Wa}} or {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.

The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}

The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Nukatabe Hirafu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika Era}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamu-no-Kuro Maro}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新羅使]][[zh:新羅使]]
'''Silla Envoys''' (新羅使, Shiragi-shi) were official delegations sent from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} to {{ill|Wa (name of Japan)|en|Wa (name of Japan)|WD=Q1378053|lt=Wa|1=Wa (name of Japan)}} or {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
== Overview ==
The dispatch of envoys began in the late 6th century, around 562 CE, when {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} occupied and destroyed the {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} administrative region.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条</ref> According to volumes 19 and 20 of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}:
{{quotation| # On the first day of the seventh lunar month of autumn, Silla sent an envoy bearing tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of winter, Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 11月条</ref> # In the eleventh lunar month of 574 (3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu|1=Emperor Bidatsu}}), Silla sent an envoy with tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 11月条</ref>}}
In particular, in 575 CE (4th year of Emperor Bidatsu's reign), it is recorded that Silla sent tribute from four villages: {{ill|Tatara|ja|多多羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sunara|ja|須奈羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Wada|ja|和陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Hachikui|ja|発鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachikui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇4th year 6月条</ref> These locations correspond to the four villages mentioned in 529 CE during Emperor Keitai’s reign: Tatara, Sunara, Wata, and Hochi.<ref>『日本書紀』継体天皇23rd year 4月条</ref> These were territories that {{ill|Ōmi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} had lost, and Silla assumed responsibility for delivering their tribute on behalf of the now-abolished Mimana.
The Yamato court allied with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, and Silla adopted a form of dual tribute, presenting its own offerings alongside Mimana’s. In 611 CE (18th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}), when Silla and Mimana’s envoys arrived, the Yamato court designated officials like {{ill|Nukatabe Hirafu|ja|額田部比羅夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukatabe Hirafu|1=Q55050985|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55050985}} and {{ill|Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|ja|膳大伴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwade no Omi Otomo|1=Q55536854|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55536854}} to oversee their reception, ensuring separate accommodations for each delegation.<ref>『日本書紀』推古天皇18th year 7月条, 9月条, 10月8日条, 9日条, 17日条, 23日条</ref>
In 642 CE, [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] regained control of Mimana's central regions from Silla. By 645 CE (1st year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika Era|1=Taika (era)}}), when tribute was offered by Silla, Baekje, and Goguryeo, it was noted:
{{quotation|Baekje's tribute envoy also served as the envoy for Mimana, delivering Mimana's tribute.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化元年7月10日条</ref>}}
This arrangement led to new measures by 646 CE, including taking hostages from Silla:
{{quotation|We dispatched the {{ill|Shōtoku|ja|小徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku|1=Q11460774|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460774}}, scholar {{ill|Takamuko no Kuromaro|en|Takamuko no Kuromaro|WD=Q1082691|lt=Takamu-no-Kuro Maro|1=Takamuko no Kuromaro}}, to Silla to take hostages, and subsequently ceased tribute collection from Mimana.<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇 大化2nd year 9月条</ref>}}
In 647 CE, {{ill|Kim Chun-chu|ja|金春秋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kim Chun-chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武烈王|1=Muyeol of Silla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494525}} was sent as a hostage to Japan. Silla continued its tribute while negotiating its position. Diplomatic ties were interrupted during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang|1=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 CE but resumed in 668 CE (7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}).<ref>『日本書紀』天智天皇7th year 9月12日条, 26日条, 29日条</ref>
== Later Developments ==
The number of Silla envoys exceeded those of the {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|WD=Q262529|lt=Japanese missions to Tang China|1=Japanese missions to Tang China}}, with 20 recorded in the 8th-9th centuries. Meanwhile, Japan sent 17 envoys to Silla. Political tensions escalated during the 8th century as Silla, now seeking equal status with Japan, renamed itself "Wangseongguk" (王城国) in 735 CE (7th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}}). This renaming led to diplomatic confrontations, including the rejection of Silla envoys.<ref>『続日本紀』天平7th year 2月27日条</ref>
By the late 8th century, formal diplomatic relations with Silla ended in 779 CE (10th year of {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|WD=Q18520676|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki}}). Afterward, relations grew increasingly strained, culminating in incidents like the rebellion of 700 Silla residents in Japan’s {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province|1=Tōtōmi Province}} and {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} in 820 CE (11th year of {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin|1=Kōnin (era)}}).<ref>『日本後紀』巻第二十八 嵯峨天皇 弘仁11th year 2月13日条</ref>
== Trade Items ==
Trade between Silla and Japan included ginseng, pine nuts, honey, ceramics, carpets, and goods from Tang China, the Western Regions, and the Southern Seas. These items ranged from fragrances and dyes to medicinal supplies.
